role_summary: First African-American Chief Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since Reconstruction (1994-2000); defense counsel for the Friendship 9 in 1961 and again at their 2015 exoneration; founding chair of the South Carolina Legislative Black Caucus (1973-75).
ai_reading: Finney's top semantic neighbors are Robert L. Carter (entry 108, NAACP LDF lawyer) at 158 and Matthew J. Perry Jr. (entry 87, South Carolina civil rights attorney) at 156, then Robert G. Clark Jr. (entry 107), Cecil J. Williams (entry 14), and Charles F. McDew (entry 16). The cluster is tightly South Carolina civil-rights-legal: two NAACP-allied SC lawyers (Carter, Perry), the SC SNCC chairman (McDew), the SC photographer who documented their movement (Williams), and a SC legislator (Clark). On the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle axis Finney sits at +0.70, consistent with his Virginia birth and South Carolina judicial career. The embedding reads him as a node in a state-level civil rights legal network that the cultural record's national-pantheon framing ("first Black Chief Justice since Reconstruction") tends to abstract away from; the catalog's precomputed semantic-overlap data restores the local-network reading.
biographical_paragraph: In December 1994, Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr. became the first African-American Chief Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since the end of Reconstruction, holding the post until his retirement on March 23, 2000, his sixty-ninth birthday [src: 1]. He had reached the court along the long civil rights apprenticeship of a Southern Black lawyer: a 1954 law degree at South Carolina State College, election to the Third Judicial Circuit bench in 1976, and an associate-justice seat on the state supreme court from 1985 [src: 1]. His most cited civil-rights case came earlier, in 1961, when he defended the Friendship 9, the Black junior-college students arrested for trying to desegregate the McCrory's lunch counter in Rock Hill, South Carolina; fifty-four years later, in 2015, he represented the surviving members of that group when a state court vacated the original convictions [src: 1]. Finney was born March 23, 1931 in Smithfield, Virginia, took his Bachelor's at Claflin College in 1952, and as a state legislator from 1972 chaired the House Judiciary Committee (the first African-American to do so in modern South Carolina) and was the charter chairperson of the Legislative Black Caucus from 1973 to 1975 [src: 1]. His daughter Nikky Finney is a poet at the University of South Carolina [src: 1]. He died of complications of Alzheimer's disease on December 3, 2017 in Columbia, South Carolina [src: 1]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 39 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr. oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
